text: Money is brass, in old coin: pence (d), shillings (twelve pence each) and pounds (240 pence). A newcomer starts with five shillings (5s).

You buy and sell with villagers: press T to talk, then use the Buy and Sell buttons. Different folk deal in different goods, and prices change from place to place: a thing is cheap where it is made and dear where it is wanted.
Selling on the spot to a villager pays a poor drop-in price, well under the going rate, and each villager only carries so much brass at a time. For real money, ship by rail.

Respected or Treasured players can use the Trust option at vendors to spot-sell Jet gems, Ammonites, Gryphaeas, and Iron Ingots at full value, bypassing the standard drop-in penalty and vendor purse limits.

Coal is cheap at the pit villages and dearer at the coast; sea fish is worth more the further inland it travels; wool sells well at Whitby and Pickering. Carrying goods from where they are cheap to where they are dear is the whole game of trade.

For the full price, right-click a station notice board and choose the Goods Market tab. It shows what you carry, which town down the line pays best, and books a rail shipment. A wagon holds 96 at once, and the brass arrives with the train about half a day later.

One steam train works the whole line. To ride, right-click a station notice board, open the Departures tab, pick where you are bound and pay the fare in coal lumps, then be stood on the platform when she calls.

You can drive the train yourself: when she stands at your platform the board offers "Take the regulator". W opens the regulator, S brakes, F shovels coal to keep her steam up, and E brings her to a stand.
To get a sheepdog, find a wild dog about the moor edge, hold meat it likes (mutton, beef or grouse) and right-click to feed it a few times until it throws its lot in with you.

With your dog out and loose sheep near, whistle her with the arrow keys: left for come-bye and right for away send her round either side, up for walk-on presses her in, down for lie-down settles her, and H brings her to heel.

Build a fold from Sheep Hurdles (one plank and two sticks makes three) and a Field Gate (two planks and two sticks). Fence a closed ring with one gate in it: the gate lets a beast walk in but never back out, while you pass through freely.

Drive a flock through the field gate and every sheep that steps inside becomes your kept stock: it takes a name, grazes at home and is there whenever you come back. You can also keep a tamed beast by fencing it in where it stands.
Once you pen 5 head of droveable stock in a fold, register your farm for a 240d charter fee at the Moorstead notice board to unlock droving.
Drive a flock to the Moorstead notice board to sell them. Sheep sell for 120d, Dale Cows for 340d, Moorland Ponies for 540d, and Pack Llamas for 110d.
Saddleback Pigs cannot be herded, but they can be sold individually at the notice board for 150d. Wild pigs drop raw pork which can be roasted for food.
Dale Bulls are aggressive and cannot be herded or tamed. Watch out for them on the open moor.
Pack Llamas can be tamed by feeding them bilberries, and they are droveable once kept in a fold.
Stake a claim to hold a plot for a fee and weekly upkeep. Active claims protect your builds from decay. Unclaimed builds decay after 30 days.
If a claim lapses, its builds crumble gradually over time. In the bairns world, claims and builds decay twice as slow.
If you keep 2 or more of the same animal species in an active claim, they may breed a tamed baby offspring over time.

At night the barghest and boggarts walk the open moor. Carry or place torches (one stick and one coal makes four) to keep the dark off, shelter in a village or a stone moor shelter, and remember that village and station ground is always safe.

Sleep the night away by finding a roof and a light: a house, the pub, a moor shelter or your own cottage with a torch in it: and pressing N. You wake at morning with full health. On the shared moor, night only passes when everyone sleeps.

Every few days a thick fog comes down on the high tops for half a day and takes the map and bearings with it. Stop, follow a wall or a waymark stone, or lay torches as breadcrumbs until it lifts. The dales and coast stay clear.

Hunger drains as you work; eat with right-click (roast mutton is the best filler). Health mends on its own when you are well fed, or fully after a night’s sleep. Dying loses you nothing: you wake on the green with your pockets intact.
Half-wild moor ponies graze the open heather. Walk up and right-click to climb on, ride with WASD (she leaps low walls herself), and press F to get down: she waits where you leave her.
A coble (a Yorkshire fishing boat) is moored at Whitby and Staithes. Right-click to climb aboard, W rows and you steer by looking, and F steps ashore. She only goes where there is water under her.
Fish from a harbour or, for fuller hauls, from a boat out in the deep sea. Now and then something snags from the sea bed: sea-washed jet, a snakestone, or sea-coal.
Dig ore with a pick: coal is shallow, ironstone is deeper (smelt it to ingots), and Whitby jet is the deepest and most precious. Iron and jet are richest near the Rosedale ironstone kilns.
Place a Mine Entrance and buy a Mining Licence for 200d to open a 10m deep shaft. Upgrade depth on the notice board up to 40m for a fee.
Designated public quarries at Moorstead, Goathland, and Pickering allow free deep digging, and the building stone there regenerates over time.
You cannot dig deeper than 1 block below the original terrain grade unless you are inside a public quarry, an old working (cave drift or scattered moor pit), or a licensed mine.

Natural caves and the old log-framed quarry pits scattered on the moor are free to mine without a licence, but their seams are worked out — sparse ore. A fresh licensed mine reaches the deeper, unworked seams.
Deeper mine bands need better picks and safety gear. Band 2 (11-20m) needs a Gritstone Pick and Pit Props; Band 3 (21-30m) needs an Iron Pick and a Safety Lamp; Band 4 (31-40m) needs a Winch.
New ores include Alum Shale (coast cliffs), Rock Salt (deep northeast), and Polyhalite (deep northeast). They are cheap at Staithes and dear inland.
Mining precious ores requires prospecting skill. Mining Jet requires Level 3, and Polyhalite requires Level 6. Novices receive only bare stone.
A range cooks and smelts. Right-click it and stoke the firebox first (a lump of coal is worth four goes), then smelt ironstone to iron ingots or roast mutton and grouse.
Press E to craft. Planks, sticks and a joiner’s bench can be made anywhere; better tools and fittings need you stood by a bench. There is a free bench and range in the pub on the green.
Tools come in tiers: wood, then gritstone, then iron: each digging faster and lasting longer. Stone and ore need a pick, trees an axe, soil a spade, and beasts respect a sword.
The old croft at the south-east of the green is yours. Raise walls, a thatch roof, a window and a light, and the village watches your progress: your standing rises with every stage you finish.

Right-click a villager to talk: they remember you between visits. Chatting and good gifts grow friendship from Stranger up to Close friend, and your standing across the parish opens better swaps and bigger ventures.
Villagers offer jobs in conversation, and the parish notice board (press Q) lists deliveries, bounties and old riddles. Finished work pays in goods, tools and standing.

Two storylines run across the real landmarks: the Hound of the Mires (something is taking sheep at night: start with Farmer James) and Count Dracula (start at the Whitby Dracula Museum; a holy-water stake fells him).
The settlements are Moorstead, Goathland, Rosedale Abbey, Staithes, Pickering, Grosmont and Whitby on the coast: all on or near the railway. The minimap names where you stand, north up.
Landmarks worth the trek: Roseberry Topping, the Hole of Horcum, the Wainstones, the Rosedale ironstone kilns, the moor crosses (say hello to Fat Betty) and the ruined abbey above Whitby.

Move with WASD, jump with Space, sprint with Z (it outruns a barghest), sneak with Shift. Left-click digs or strikes, right-click places, eats, talks and uses boards. E opens your pockets, Q the journal.
Merlin is the wizard of the moor. Say his name in chat (press T) from anywhere and he comes to you: he leads you to places, points the way, and works spells if you bring him the right token.
